literal: organ of knowledge, sense of perception; *jñāna* 'knowledge, cognition' + *indriya* 'sense-organ, faculty', hence 'the faculty by which knowledge of objects arises' — a sense of perception (also *buddhīndriya*, 'organ of cognition')
invariant_definition: *Jñānendriya* (also *buddhīndriya*) is an organ of knowledge — one of the five senses of perception by which cognition of objects arises: hearing, touch, sight, taste, and smell (ear, skin, eye, tongue, nose). It is one of the two classes into which the Sāṅkhya analysis divides the ten external *indriyas*, the other being the five *karmendriyas*, the organs of action. Together with *manas* (which Sāṅkhya counts as an eleventh, common to both classes) these make up the instruments through which the embodied self transacts with the world. The five *jñānendriyas* are evolutes of *ahaṅkāra* in its *sāttvika* aspect and are not the physical organs (the *golakas*) but the subtle perceptive faculties seated in them. In Vedānta the same scheme is taken up: the *jñānendriyas* are the channels through which sense-objects reach the inner instrument, and their governance — drawing them in from their objects (*pratyāhāra*) — is central to the discipline of the *sthitaprajña* and the yogin. The class is defined functionally, by the cognition it yields, in contrast to the *karmendriyas*, which are defined by the action they perform.

school_framing.shared_core: *Jñānendriya* names **a sense of perception — one of the five organs of knowledge (hearing, touch, sight, taste, smell)** by which cognition of objects arises, as against the *karmendriyas* of action. The fivefold scheme is shared across Sāṅkhya, Yoga, and Vedānta.
school_framing.register_axes_note: Broadly **shared** as a term of psychological analysis. Differences concern the wider ontology in which the senses are placed (evolutes of *ahaṅkāra* in Sāṅkhya–Yoga; instruments of the self under *upādhi* in Advaita), not the identity of the five perceptive faculties.

per_school.definition: The five *jñānendriyas* (*buddhīndriyas*) — ear, skin, eye, tongue, nose — are, with the five *karmendriyas* and *manas*, the eleven *indriyas* that evolve from *ahaṅkāra* (Sāṅkhya-Kārikā 24–26). The organs of perception have hearing, touch, seeing, tasting, and smelling as their functions; they are subtle faculties, not the gross physical organs, and operate together with *manas*, *ahaṅkāra*, and *buddhi* to present objects to the *puruṣa*. They are defined by the cognition they yield, the *karmendriyas* by their action.
primary_loci: Sāṅkhya-Kārikā 28 (the function of the perceptive organs)
per_school.register_tag: M+E; reflective reader (the five perceptive faculties)

per_school.definition: Advaita adopts the Sāṅkhya enumeration: the five *jñānendriyas* are channels through which sense-objects reach the inner instrument (*antaḥkaraṇa*), themselves evolutes of the *sāttvika* aspect of the elements. They belong to the *upādhi*-conditioned apparatus of the *jīva*, not to the *ātman*, which is their witness. Their restraint and inward withdrawal is integral to the discipline that prepares for knowledge; the self is realized precisely by discriminating it from the senses and their cognitions.
primary_loci: Vedānta-Sāra (the jñānendriyas among the seventeen-fold subtle body)
Pañcadaśī (the indriyas as instruments of the jīva, witnessed by ātman)

translator_note: Render 'organ of knowledge', 'sense of perception', or 'perceptive faculty'; the synonym *buddhīndriya* names the same five. Keep clear that these are the subtle faculties, not the physical organs (*golaka*): the eye as *jñānendriya* is the power of sight, not the eyeball. The class is defined by the cognition it produces, in deliberate contrast to the *karmendriyas* (organs of action), and the two together with *manas* make up the standard set of eleven *indriyas*.
